posts.title: 5 Steps to a Smooth Excess Soil Management Plan
posts.excerpt: A well-prepared Excess Soil Management Plan keeps your project on schedule and on the right side of O. Reg. 406/19. Here is the proven five-step process our QPs follow on every project.
posts.body:
Managing excess soil on an Ontario construction project is no longer a matter of calling a trucking company and pointing them down the road. Since O. Reg. 406/19 came into force, every cubic metre of displaced soil must be tracked, characterized, and documented before it leaves your site. The good news: a solid Excess Soil Management Plan (ESMP) turns a potential compliance headache into a well-oiled logistics workflow.

Here is the five-step process our Qualified Persons (QPs) follow on every project.

---

## Step 1 — Determine Your Project Class

The first question is always: *what class of project do you have?*

- **Class 1** projects are large-scale infrastructure and development projects. They require a full ESMP registered in the Ontario Excess Soil Registry before any soil leaves the site.
- **Class 2** projects are smaller in scope and carry a simplified, but still mandatory, documentation burden.

Getting the classification wrong is the single fastest way to trigger a stop-work order. A QP will review your site area, the nature of the work, and applicable thresholds to make the call early — before a single bucket of earth is touched.

![Aerial view of a construction site with excavated soil stockpiles](https://lirp.cdn-website.com/b7deef0f/dms3rep/multi/opt/constrtuction+gorunds-1920w.jpg)

---

## Step 2 — Soil Characterization

Before soil can move anywhere, you need to know what is in it. A **Soil Characterization Report** involves collecting samples from representative areas of the project area and testing them against the applicable Standards Table under O. Reg. 153/04.

The results determine:

- Which quality standard the soil meets (e.g., Table 1 Residential, Table 2 Industrial/Commercial)
- Whether the soil qualifies as **clean fill** or requires a designated receiving site
- Any special handling or notification requirements

![Soil sampling technician collecting cores on a project site](https://lirp.cdn-website.com/b7deef0f/dms3rep/multi/opt/soil_sampling-94e1ea1c-1920w.jpg)

Skipping or shortcutting characterization is the most common compliance failure we see. Undocumented or misclassified soil at a receiving site can expose the generator — that means *you*, the constructor — to significant regulatory and financial liability.

---

## Step 3 — Identify and Qualify Receiving Sites

Once you know your soil quality, you need somewhere appropriate to send it. Every receiving site must:

1. Complete an **Excess Soil Destination Assessment Report (ESDAR)** confirming the site is suitable to receive soil at the proposed quality standard
2. Be registered in the Ontario Excess Soil Registry *before* your first load arrives
3. Have an authorized QP on file

This step is where projects stall most often. Lining up multiple qualified receiving sites — each with their own ESDAR and Registry listing — takes time. Start this process in parallel with characterization, not after.

![Fill management site receiving a truck load of clean excavated material](https://lirp.cdn-website.com/b7deef0f/dms3rep/multi/opt/Fill+Management+Plan-2880w.jpg)

---

## Step 4 — Register the ESMP and Begin Tracking

With characterization complete and receiving sites qualified, your QP prepares and registers the full ESMP in the **Ontario Excess Soil Registry**. The plan includes:

- Project area description and project class determination
- Soil volume estimates by quality standard
- Receiving site details and maximum tonnage allocations
- Hauling and tracking procedures
- QP sign-off and professional seal

From this point forward, every load must be tracked. Load tracking records — truck ID, volume, origin location, destination site, and driver signature — must be kept for a minimum of five years.

---

## Step 5 — On-Site Oversight and Closeout

Compliance does not end when the first truck rolls out. Throughout the project your QP should:

- Conduct periodic field reviews to confirm soil is being loaded from the correct characterization zones
- Review load tracking records for accuracy and completeness
- Respond to any unexpected finds (e.g., discoloured soil or odours) with supplementary sampling
- Prepare a **project closeout report** confirming all soil was managed in accordance with the registered ESMP

![Soil management systems in action — bulldozer managing stockpiles on a large site](https://lirp.cdn-website.com/b7deef0f/dms3rep/multi/opt/Soil+Management+Strategies-2880w.jpg)

---

## The Bottom Line

A properly executed ESMP protects your project timeline, limits your liability, and keeps regulators satisfied. The five steps above are not bureaucratic obstacles — they are the fastest path from groundbreaking to completion without an enforcement surprise.

XS Soil Solutions provides end-to-end ESMP services across Ontario. Our QPs are on the ground, know the Registry inside and out, and have helped hundreds of projects move soil cleanly and compliantly.

**Ready to get started?** Contact us at [info@xssoil.ca](mailto:info@xssoil.ca) or call [905-334-1197](tel:9053341197) for a free project assessment.

posts.title: Understanding O. Reg. 406/19: What Ontario Contractors Need to Know
posts.excerpt: Ontario's excess soil regulation continues to evolve. Here's what constructors, developers, and property owners need to know to stay compliant.
posts.body:
## What is O. Reg. 406/19?

Ontario's On-Site and Excess Soil Management regulation came into force in January 2021, fundamentally changing how excess soil is managed across the province. Whether you're a constructor breaking ground on a new subdivision, a developer managing multiple sites, or a municipality handling road rehabilitation, this regulation almost certainly applies to you.

![Excavated material stockpiled on a large infrastructure project](https://lirp.cdn-website.com/b7deef0f/dms3rep/multi/opt/Excavated+Material+Management+Plan-2880w.webp)

## Who Does It Apply To?

The regulation applies to any project that generates excess soil — soil that will be removed from the project area. There are two project classes:

- **Class 1 projects** — large-scale construction and infrastructure projects with the most comprehensive requirements
- **Class 2 projects** — smaller projects with a simplified but still mandatory set of requirements

## Key Compliance Requirements

- Excess Soil Management Plans (ESMPs) are mandatory for Class 1 projects
- All soil lots must be registered in the Ontario Excess Soil Registry
- Qualified Persons (QPs) must author and sign all key documents
- Receiving sites must complete an Excess Soil Destination Assessment before accepting any fill

![Soil sampling being conducted to characterize excess material](https://lirp.cdn-website.com/b7deef0f/dms3rep/multi/opt/soil_sampling-94e1ea1c-1920w.jpg)

## Common Mistakes to Avoid

1. Moving soil before an ESMP is registered
2. Using an unqualified consultant to prepare regulatory documents
3. Failing to register receiving sites before accepting fill
4. Incomplete chain-of-custody documentation during hauling

## How XS Soil Solutions Can Help

Not sure if your project is compliant? Our Qualified Persons provide free project assessments and can have an ESMP registered in as little as five business days for straightforward projects.

Contact us at [info@xssoil.ca](mailto:info@xssoil.ca) or call [905-334-1197](tel:9053341197).
